Respiratory tract, sputum, bronchial brushing, or lavage specimen
PSC Category II: Negative — acute neutrophilic inflammation
Singles and loose aggregates in mucus show segmented multilobed nuclei with dense clumped chromatin, inconspicuous nucleoli, high N:C ratio, and scant pale finely granular cytoplasm.
Mucopurulent exudate, fibrin and bacteria; CD15, CD66b, myeloperoxidase positive; cytokeratin negative.
Multilobed segmented nucleus distinguishes this vs Lymphocyte, which has a single round dense nucleus and thin basophilic cytoplasmic rim.
Respiratory tract, sputum or bronchoalveolar lavage specimen
PSC Category II: Negative — alveolar macrophages

Singly dispersed or loose clusters of histiocytes show eccentric oval or bean-shaped nuclei, fine chromatin, inconspicuous nucleoli, low N:C ratio, and abundant foamy vacuolated cytoplasm with carbon or hemosiderin.
Watery mucus with dust, hemosiderin or debris; CD68, CD163 positive; epithelial markers negative.
Pigment-laden foamy cytoplasm distinguishes this vs Adenocarcinoma, which forms glandular clusters with malignant nucleoli and mucin vacuoles.
Respiratory tract, bronchial brushing or washing specimen
PSC Category II: Negative — benign bronchial epithelium

Cohesive sheets, strips, and picket-fence groups show basally oriented uniform oval nuclei, smooth membranes, fine chromatin, low-to-moderate N:C ratio, and delicate basophilic columnar cytoplasm with apical cilia.
Clean mucus with goblet cells; CK7, TTF-1 variably positive; p40 limited to basal cells.
Apical cilia and terminal bars distinguish this vs Adenocarcinoma, which lacks cilia and forms disordered three-dimensional glandular clusters.
Respiratory tract, bronchoalveolar lavage or lymph-node FNA specimen
PSC Category II: Negative — benign lymphoid cells
Small dispersed mononuclear cells show round uniform nuclei with dense clumped mature chromatin, absent nucleoli, very high N:C ratio, and a scant thin basophilic cytoplasmic rim.
Reactive lymphoid background; CD45 positive with mixed CD3 T cells and CD20 B cells.
Uniform small nuclei without molding distinguish this vs Small cell carcinoma, which shows nuclear molding, crush artifact, and neuroendocrine chromatin.
Respiratory specimen, smear or liquid-based cytology preparation
PSC Category I: Nondiagnostic — contaminant or artifact

Amorphous granular, filamentous, refractile, or angular noncellular material lacks true cellular arrangement, nuclear membranes, chromatin, nucleoli, N:C relationship, and definable cytoplasmic borders.
Acellular mucus, stain precipitate, fibers, talc or lubricant; IHC noncontributory, no cytokeratin/CD45/CD68 staining.
Complete absence of nuclear membrane and cytoplasm distinguishes this vs Macrophages, which show true foamy cytoplasm and eccentric nuclei.
Respiratory tract, sputum or bronchial brushing specimen
PSC Category II: Negative — benign squamous epithelium

Flat sheets and isolated polygonal cells show small central or pyknotic nuclei, smooth contours, condensed chromatin, low N:C ratio, and abundant dense cyanophilic, eosinophilic, or orangeophilic cytoplasm.
Upper-airway contamination or metaplasia; p40, p63, CK5/6 positive; Ki-67 low.
Bland pyknotic nuclei with abundant sharp-bordered cytoplasm distinguish this vs Squamous cell carcinoma, which shows tumor diathesis and pleomorphic keratinization.
Respiratory tract, bronchial brushing, washing, or FNA specimen
PSC Category III: Atypical — reactive/indeterminate epithelial atypia

Scattered cells or loose clusters show partial loss of polarity, cytomegaly, mild anisonucleosis, smooth-to-slightly irregular membranes, fine-to-mildly coarse chromatin, and vacuolated or metaplastic cytoplasm.
Inflammation, necrotic debris, therapy effect or infection; lineage CK7/p40/TTF-1 retained, Ki-67 not high.
Limited mild anisonucleosis with retained lineage pattern distinguishes this vs PSC Category V Suspicious, which has quantitatively stronger malignant atypia.
Serous cavity, pleural/peritoneal/pericardial effusion cytology specimen
TIS Category II: NFM — negative for malignancy
Benign mesothelial cells occur singly, in flat sheets, or small windows-bearing groups with round uniform nuclei, fine chromatin, occasional nucleoli, low N:C ratio, and dense two-tone cytoplasm.
Proteinaceous fluid with lymphocytes/macrophages; calretinin, WT1, D2-40 positive; claudin-4, BerEP4 negative.
Intercellular windows and smooth even nuclei distinguish this vs Metastatic adenocarcinoma, which forms tight morules with claudin-4 positivity.
Serous cavity, pleural/peritoneal/pericardial effusion cytology specimen
TIS Category III: AUS — atypia of undetermined significance

Reactive clusters or limited papillary/pseudoglandular groups show karyomegaly, mild chromatin irregularity, smooth-to-slightly irregular membranes, prominent nucleoli, and abundant dense or vacuolated mesothelial cytoplasm.
Serositis with blood or inflammation; mesothelial calretinin/WT1 positive; claudin-4 absent or equivocal.
Mild karyomegaly with mesothelial windows distinguishes this vs Malignant effusion, which shows tight three-dimensional epithelial clusters and claudin-4 positivity.
Thyroid gland, ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration specimen
Bethesda VI: Malignant — papillary thyroid carcinoma

Papillary fragments, syncytial sheets, and micropapillae show enlarged oval overlapping nuclei, powdery chromatin, grooves, intranuclear pseudoinclusions, high N:C ratio, and delicate cyanophilic cytoplasm.
Scant colloid, psammoma bodies, giant cells; CK19, HBME-1, galectin-3, TTF-1, PAX8 positive.
True intranuclear pseudoinclusions with grooves distinguish this vs Follicular neoplasm, which lacks diffuse PTC nuclear membrane abnormalities.
Thyroid gland, ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration specimen
Bethesda V: Suspicious for malignancy — suspicious PTC

Limited cellularity with papillaroid clusters or crowded sheets shows focal enlarged oval nuclei, occasional grooves or pseudoinclusions, pale chromatin, increased N:C ratio, and scant delicate cytoplasm.
Scant colloid; CK19, HBME-1, galectin-3 may support PTC; BRAF VE1 optional.
Focal PTC-type nuclear changes distinguish this vs Bethesda VI PTC, which requires a quantitatively convincing malignant follicular-cell population.
Thyroid gland, ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration specimen
Bethesda I: Nondiagnostic — scant follicular cells
Blood, cyst fluid, macrophages, or colloid predominate with absent or too few follicular cell groups; nuclei are absent or bland, and cytoplasm is foamy, degenerative, or obscured.
Cyst fluid, blood, clotting or drying artifact; IHC not informative; thyroglobulin highlights rare follicular cells.
Insufficient benign follicular groups distinguish this vs Bethesda II Benign, which shows adequate colloid-associated follicular epithelium.
Thyroid gland, cyst-fluid fine-needle aspiration specimen
Bethesda I: Nondiagnostic — cyst fluid only

Singly dispersed or loose histiocytic clusters show eccentric oval nuclei, smooth membranes, fine chromatin, inconspicuous nucleoli, low N:C ratio, and abundant foamy vacuolated cytoplasm with hemosiderin.
Cyst fluid, old hemorrhage and colloid; CD68/CD163 positive; thyroglobulin, TTF-1 negative.
Foamy hemosiderin-laden cytoplasm distinguishes this vs Follicular epithelial cells, which form cohesive sheets and express thyroglobulin.
Thyroid gland, ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration specimen
Bethesda III: AUS — atypia of undetermined significance

Sparsely cellular microfollicles or crowded trabecular groups show mild nuclear enlargement, pale chromatin, rare grooves, slight membrane irregularity, and scant-to-moderate cytoplasm, sometimes oncocytic granular.
Scant colloid or Hürthle-cell change; molecular testing optional; CK19/HBME-1 not definitively diagnostic.
Rare grooves or limited microfollicles distinguish this vs Bethesda IV Follicular neoplasm, which shows a predominant microfollicular pattern.
Thyroid gland, ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration specimen
Bethesda II: Benign — follicular nodular disease
Flat honeycomb sheets and macrofollicular groups show uniform round-to-oval nuclei, smooth membranes, fine granular chromatin, inconspicuous nucleoli, low N:C ratio, and moderate delicate cytoplasm.
Abundant watery or thick colloid, macrophages possible; thyroglobulin, TTF-1, PAX8 positive in follicular cells.
Abundant colloid with honeycomb sheets distinguishes this vs Follicular neoplasm, which is cellular and microfollicular with scant colloid.
Thyroid gland, fine-needle aspiration smear specimen
Bethesda II: Benign — follicular epithelial cells
Cohesive monolayer sheets, honeycomb groups, and small follicles show round uniform centrally placed nuclei, smooth membranes, fine chromatin, low-to-moderate N:C ratio, and delicate pale cytoplasm.
Colloid-rich thyroid aspirate; follicular cells express thyroglobulin, TTF-1, PAX8; calcitonin negative.
Orderly honeycomb follicular architecture distinguishes this vs Papillary thyroid carcinoma, which shows crowding, grooves, pseudoinclusions, and powdery chromatin.
Urinary tract, voided urine or bladder washing specimen
Paris II: NHGUC — negative for high-grade urothelial carcinoma
Benign urothelial cells, umbrella cells, and squamous cells form flat sheets or small clusters with smooth oval nuclei, fine chromatin, inconspicuous nucleoli, low N:C ratio, and abundant cytoplasm.
Urine matrix with inflammation/instrumentation; GATA3, uroplakin positive; CK20 limited, p53 wild-type, Ki-67 low.
Low N:C ratio with smooth chromatin distinguishes this vs AUC, which requires increased N:C ratio and hyperchromatic irregular nuclei.
Urinary tract, voided urine or bladder washing specimen
Paris IV: SHGUC — suspicious for high-grade urothelial carcinoma
Few severely atypical urothelial cells occur singly or in small clusters with high N:C ratio above 0.7, hyperchromasia, coarse chromatin, irregular nuclear contours, and scant dense cytoplasm.
Hematuric or necrotic urine; CK20 diffuse, p53 aberrant, Ki-67 high may support HGUC.
Few high-grade cells distinguish this vs HGUC, which requires many similarly abnormal cells with convincing high-grade morphology.
Urinary tract, voided urine or bladder washing specimen
Paris III: AUC — atypical urothelial cells
Scattered urothelial cells or small groups show increased N:C ratio above 0.5, mild hyperchromasia, irregular nuclear contours or clumpy chromatin, and scant-to-moderate cytoplasm.
Inflammation, stones, instrumentation or therapy effect common; CK20/p53/Ki-67 adjunctive but not definitive.
Increased N:C ratio with only one high-grade criterion distinguishes this vs SHGUC, which has high N:C ratio and multiple severe features.
Urinary tract, voided urine or bladder washing specimen
Paris V: HGUC — high-grade urothelial carcinoma
Many dyscohesive cells and irregular clusters show N:C ratio above 0.7, marked hyperchromasia, coarse clumpy chromatin, irregular nuclear membranes, occasional nucleoli, and scant dense cytoplasm.
Necrotic or bloody urine; GATA3, p63, uroplakin positive; CK20 diffuse, p53 aberrant, Ki-67 high.
Numerous cells meeting high N:C and severe nuclear criteria distinguish this vs SHGUC, which has too few diagnostic cells.
Uterine cervix, liquid-based ThinPrep cervical cytology specimen
Bethesda NILM: negative for intraepithelial lesion or malignancy
Superficial and intermediate squamous cells form flat sheets with small pyknotic or vesicular smooth nuclei, fine chromatin, low N:C ratio, and abundant transparent cyanophilic cytoplasm.
Clean mucus with lactobacilli; p16 negative/non-block, Ki-67 limited to basal metaplastic cells.
Low N:C ratio and smooth nuclei distinguish this vs ASC-US, which shows nuclear enlargement beyond reactive expectations.
Uterine cervix, liquid-based ThinPrep cervical cytology specimen
Bethesda ASC-US: atypical squamous cells, undetermined significance

Mature squamous cells occur singly or in sheets with nuclear enlargement about 2.5–3 times normal, mild hyperchromasia, slight membrane irregularity, and abundant cytoplasm without definitive koilocytosis.
Inflammation or atrophy may coexist; hrHPV triage recommended; p16/Ki-67 dual stain may help.
Borderline nuclear enlargement without true koilocytosis distinguishes this vs LSIL, which has sharply defined perinuclear halos and irregular hyperchromatic nuclei.
Uterine cervix, liquid-based ThinPrep cervical cytology specimen
Bethesda ASC-H: atypical squamous cells, cannot exclude HSIL
Small metaplastic-like cells in singles or crowded fragments show high N:C ratio, hyperchromatic irregular nuclei, coarse chromatin, absent nucleoli, and scant dense cyanophilic cytoplasm.
Atrophy or metaplasia may confound; block p16 and increased Ki-67 support HSIL.
Focal high N:C atypical cells distinguish this vs HSIL, where abnormal immature squamous cells are quantitatively sufficient.
Uterine cervix, liquid-based ThinPrep cervical cytology specimen
Bethesda LSIL: low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion

Mature superficial/intermediate squamous cells show enlarged hyperchromatic irregular nuclei at least three times normal, binucleation, coarse chromatin, low-to-moderate N:C ratio, and abundant cytoplasm with koilocytotic halos.
Koilocytic inflammatory background; HPV effect present, p16 variable/patchy, Ki-67 mildly expanded.
Koilocytotic halo with nuclear atypia distinguishes this vs ASC-US, which lacks definitive HPV cytopathic nuclear changes.
Uterine cervix, liquid-based ThinPrep cervical cytology specimen
Bethesda HSIL/SCC: high-grade SIL or squamous carcinoma

Small immature cells in syncytial aggregates or singles show very high N:C ratio, hyperchromatic coarse chromatin, angular irregular nuclei, absent or macronucleoli, and scant basophilic or keratinized cytoplasm.
Tumor diathesis if invasive; p16 block positive, Ki-67 high, p40/p63 positive in SCC.
Immature high N:C cells with block p16 distinguish this vs LSIL, which has mature koilocytes and lower N:C ratio.
Uterine cervix, liquid-based cervical cytology specimen
Bethesda AGC/AIS: atypical glandular cells or adenocarcinoma

Endocervical strips, rosettes, feathered sheets, or endometrial ball-like clusters show enlarged oval pseudostratified nuclei, coarse chromatin, nucleoli, high N:C ratio, and mucinous or scant cyanophilic cytoplasm.
Mucin, blood or necrosis; p16/CEA support endocervical, ER/PAX8/vimentin support endometrial origin.
Feathering and rosettes distinguish this vs Reactive endocervical cells, which retain honeycomb polarity and smooth evenly spaced nuclei.
Female lower genital tract, cervical-vaginal cytology specimen
Bethesda NILM: Trichomonas vaginalis organism present

Pear-shaped or oval organisms lie singly among squamous cells with a small eccentric vesicular nucleus, fine chromatin, and pale finely granular cytoplasm; flagella or undulating membrane may be visible.
Neutrophilic inflammatory background and reduced lactobacilli; IHC not routine, NAAT confirms infection.
Eccentric nucleus with flagellated granular cytoplasm distinguishes this vs Neutrophil, which has multilobed nuclei and no organismal cytoplasm.
Female lower genital tract, cervical-vaginal cytology specimen
Bethesda NILM: fungal organisms consistent with Candida

Budding yeasts and elongated pseudohyphae intertwine with squamous cells; host nuclei are bland or mildly reactive, while gray-brown eosinophilic fungal cytoplasm creates spearing or shish-kebab arrays.
Neutrophilic background; PAS/GMS stains highlight yeasts and pseudohyphae; IHC is not routinely used.
Constricted pseudohyphae with budding yeasts distinguish this vs Trichomonas, which shows pear-shaped organisms with eccentric nuclei.
Female lower genital tract, cervical-vaginal cytology specimen
Bethesda NILM: bacterial vaginosis, HSV, or Actinomyces

Clue cells, multinucleated HSV-infected cells, or cotton-ball filamentous colonies show normal BV nuclei or HSV ground-glass molded nuclei with margination, and cytoplasm coated by coccobacilli or inclusions.
Coccobacillary flora, acute inflammation or IUD-associated filaments; HSV IHC positive if needed; Gram/GMS helpful.
Clue-cell coating, HSV 3M nuclei, or Actinomyces filaments distinguish this vs Candida, which shows budding yeasts and pseudohyphae.
Uterine cervix, endocervical liquid-based cytology specimen
Bethesda NILM: benign endocervical component present
Benign endocervical cells form honeycomb sheets, strips, and palisaded picket-fence groups with basally placed oval nuclei, fine chromatin, inconspicuous nucleoli, and mucinous columnar cytoplasm.
Clear mucus background; CK7, PAX8, CEA positive endocervical phenotype; p16 non-block.
Honeycomb polarity and picket-fence strips distinguish this vs AGC, which shows crowding, pseudostratification, feathering, and nuclear enlargement.
